INDIAN DENTAL ASSOCIATION
The Indian Dental Association (IDA) was formed in the year 1949.
Before that it was called the ALL India Dental Association which was
formed in 1928 by Dr. R. Ahmad
OBJECTIVES
Promotion encouragement and advancement of dental and allied
sciences
To encourage the members to undertake measures for the improvem
ent of public health and education in India
Maintenance of the honour and dignity and upholding of interests
of the dental profession and cooperation between the members there of
FUNCTIONS
Holding periodical meetings and conferences of the members of the
Publishing association and circulating a journal specially adopted to the needs
of the dental profession in India
Encouraging the opening of libraries in the branches and buying
books out of funds of the association
Publishing from Time to time papers related to dental researches
conducted by members independently or under the auspices of
association
conducting educational campaigns among the masses in India
in the matter of oral hygiene
Also to express its views on all questions pertaining to the Indian
legislation affecting public heal dental profession and
dental education
to
Protects public from unethical treatment by unqualified
practitioners.

DENTAL COUNCIL OF INDIA
Farmed in 12th sibuts April lotish 1949, the Dental Council of India is a statuatory
body mainly concerned with the maintenance of standard of dental
education and to register quacks from the field.
COMPOSITION
One registered dentist passessing a recognised dental qualification
elected by the dentists registered in part A of each state register.
One member elected from amongst themselves by the members of
the Medical Council of India'
Not more than 4 members elected from among themselves by
a> Principals, Deans, Directors and Vice-Principals of dental
colleges in the states training students for recognised dental
qualifications, provided that not more than one member
shall be elected from dental college
b> Heads of dental wings of medical colleges in the states training
students far recognised dental qualification
a
c> One member from each university established by the Law in the
states which grants a recognised dental qualification
One member to represent each state nominated by the government
of each such state from among persons registered either in a medical
or dental register of the state
Six members nominated by the Central Government of whom atleast
one shall be a registered dentist passessing a recognised dental
qualification and holding an appointment in an institution for the
training of dentists
The Director General of Health Services (ex-officio)
FUNCTIONS
Basic principles for the maintenance of minimum education
standard for BDS degree

Min physical requirements of a dental college PAPER
Min staff pattern for undergraduate dental studies in colleges
with 40, 60 and 100 admissions.
Basic qualifications and teaching experience required to teach
BDS & MDS students
General establishment of dental facilities its duration of course,
selection of students
Migration and transfer rules of for students
Regulation of scheme of examination far BDS and MDS

DENTISTS ACT OF INDIA
are IDA
The Dentists Act, 1948 is an act to regulate the profession of dentistry.
It was introduced on 29 March, 1948
The act contains five chapters and their sub-sections
CHAPTER 1- INTRODUCTION 80
1> SHORT TITLE & EXTENT
The act is called the Dentists Act 1948
It extends to the whole of India
2> INTERPRETATION OF THE ACT
"The Council" means the Dental Council of India
"Dentist" means a person who practises dentistry
"Dentistry means lot the performance of any treatment/operation
of any disease / deficiency of human teeth and jaws
It also includes the performance of radiographic work 8 of the oral
cavity and the mechanical construction / renewal of artificial
dentures
"Medical practitioner" means a person who holds a qualification
granted by an authority specified under section 3 of the Indian
Medical Degrees Act 1916.
"Prescribed "means prescribed by rules or regulations made under
this act
"State" council" means a state Dencil Council constituted under
Section 21.
CHAPTER II - DENTAL COUNCIL OF INDIA
The Dental Council of India to is Naitorilaan statuatory body which is mainly
concerned with the maintenance of standard of dental educa-
tion and to register qualified dentist and 8237 eliminate quacks
from the field.

CHANGING CONCEPTS OF HEALTH
Health is a state of complete physical ,mental and social well-being
and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity. [WHO]
The changing concepts are -
1> BIOMEDICAL CONCEPT
- In this concept, health is viewed as absence from disease
- It is based on germ theory of disease
- This concept does not consider the environmental, social and
cultural determinants of health
- Recent developments concluded that this concept is inadequate
2> ECOLOGICAL CONCEPT
- This concept views health as a dynamic equilibrium between
man and his environment and disease a maladjustment of
between them
Improvement in human adaptation to his environment can lead to
BEARDEDBONG
longer life expectancies and a better quality of Life.
3> PSYCHOSOCIAL CONCEPT
- This concept reveals that health is not just a biomedical phenomenon
but is influenced by social, psychological, cultural, economic and
political factors.
- Thus, health is considered both a biological and a social phenome
-non.
4> HOLISTIC CONCEPT
- It is a synthesis of all the above concepts and implies that all
sectors of society have an effect on health.
- It recognises the strength of social, economic, political and
environmental influences on health.
- The emphasis is on the promotion & protection of health.

CONCLUSION
To conclude health and disease Lie along a continuum and there is no
single cut off point. The lowest point on the health disease spectrum
is death and the highest point corresponds to the WHO defination of
positive health
Positive Health
Better Health
Freedom from sickness
Unrecognised sickness
Mild sickness
Severe sickness
Death
@THE BEARDEDBONG

EPIDEMIOLOGICAL TRIAD
The occurance and manifestations of any disease whether
communicable or non communicable are determined by
the interactions between the agents, hast & environment
which together constitute the epidemiological triad
AGENT
tais.
avixia
HOST
ENVIRONMENT
1> AGENT
The agent is defined as an organism, a substance or a force, the
presence. or Lack of which may initiate a disease process or may
cause it to continue.
@THE EARDEDBONG

Non Living or Inanimate

2> HOST
Hast is defined as a person or an animal that affords subsistence
or lodgement to an infectious disease under natural conditions.

The characteristics of a human being that determine how he reacts
to the agents in the environment are called hast factors.
The hast factors are,
a> Demographic characteristics - age, sex, ethnicity
b> Biological characteristics - genetic background, immune
status loaipoloid nutritional status, physiologic characteristics
c> Socio-economic characteristics - social class, religion,
education, marital status
d> Life style - Living habits, food habits etc.
3> ENVIRONMENT
Environment is the source or reservoir far the agents of disease.
It helps in the transmission of agents to the hast, bringing about their
contact and interaction.
The environment of man is of 2 types
a> Internal environment DEDBONG
- it refers to every tissue, argan organ system
their harmonious functioning within the system.
- fault in functioning of any component parts results
in disharmony or disease
b> External environment
- it refers to all that which is external to the individual
human hast.
Generally the environment can be studied under 3 headings -
a> Physical environment space around man
b> Biological environment
c> Social environment

ICEBERG PHENOMENON
Health professionals see only a small part of the illness in the
community, just as only a small part of an iceberg is visible
above the surface of water. This is called the tip of the iceberg"
as information on the submerged partion is not available.
But the inapparant cases are important for their role in transmission
Since many inapparant infections can be transmitted and can produce
disease in others it is not sufficient to direct disease management
procedures solely to clinically apparant cases.

A> EPIDEMIOLOGICAL TRIAD
The occurance and manifestations of any disease, whether communi-
cable or non communicable are determined by the interactions between
the agent the hast and the environment, which together constitute
the epidemiological triad
AGENT
@THE BEARDEDBONG
HOST
ENVIRONMENT
B> MULTIFACTORIAL CAUSATION
This concept was put by Petenkofer
It is based on the fact that modern diseases of civilisation Like
caronary heart disease and cancers could not be explained on
the basis of "single cause idea" because they were due to multiple
factors.
This concept offers multiple approaches for the prevention of disease
C> NATURAL HISTORY OF DISEASE
This signifies the way in which a disease evolves over time from the
period of its earliest stage to its termination as recovery or death
It consists of 2 phases -

Period of pre pathogenesis - The disease agent has not yet entered man,
but the factors which favor its interaction with the human hast are
present in the environment.
It refers to processess occuring in the environment
Period of pathogenesis - This phase begins with the entry of the disease
agent into the human hast. The lato final outcome may be recovery,
disability or death.
D> WEB OF CAUSATION
The term was coined by Mac Mahon and Prigh.
It is applicable in certain diseases, where the causative agent may be
unknown/uncertain and the disease is the result of interaction of
multiple factors.
Removal of some of them or even one of them (impartant link) may
@THE BEARDEDBONG
be sufficient to control disease
E> RISK FACTORS & RISK GROUPS
Risk factor means an attribute or exposure that is significantly
associated with the development of a disease.
Risk factors are offen suggestive i.e the presence of a risk factor
does not imply that the disease will occur and its absence, the
disease will not occur.
Risk groups are those who are exposed to risk factors.
FV
SPECTRUM OF DISEASE
It is a graphic representation of the variations in the manifestations
of disease.
It may be defined as the sequence of events that occur in the human
host from Time of contact with the etiologic agent upto the point of
the ultimate outcome, which may be fatal in extreme cases.

Subclinical infections

of water. This is called the tip of the iceberg as information on

PRIMARY HEALTH CARE
DEFINITION
Primary Health care is defined as essential health care based on
practical scientifically sound and socially acceptable methods and
technology made universally accessible to individuals and families in
the community through their full participation and at a cost that ex
the Trooa community and country can affard to maintain at every stage
of their development in the spirit of self determination.
PRINCIPLES OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE
1. EQUITABLE DISTRIBUTION
The first key principle of primary health care is equitable distribution
of health services i.e health services must be shared equally by all
rural must have access to health services. DBONG
people irrespective of their ability to pay and all rich poor, urban or
At present, health services are mainly concentrated in major towns
and cities and this is Termed social injustice
sifitries
Primary health care aims to redress this by shifting the centre of
gravity of health care services from cities to rural areas
2. COMMUNITY PARTICIPATION
The involvement of individuals, families and communities in promo-
tion of their own health and welfare is an essential ingredient of
primary health care.
102
The community must involve in the planning, implementation and
maintenance of health services, -13 besides maximum reliance on Local
resources.

3> INTERSECTORAL COORDINATION
Planning with other sectors is important to avoid unnecessary
duplication of activities.
100.00
The declaration of Al-ma-Ata states that "primary health care
involves in addition to the health sector all related sectors
and aspects of national and community development, 100.00 in parti-
cular agriculture animal husbandry food industry education,
housing public works
In order to achieve such cooperation the administrative system
of the country has to be reviewed, their resources reallocated &
suitable legislation introduced to ensure such coardination can
take place.
The major reasons for Lack of success of many oral health
programs is the fact that they operate in isolation, separate from
general health care system
@THE BEARDEDBONG
4> APPROPRIATE TECHNOLOGY
to to local needs, and acceptable to those who apply it and those for
It suctives is defined as technology that is scientifically sNio sound Hlass adaptable
whom it is used, and that can be maintained by the people
themselves in keeping with the principles. of self reliance with the
resources the community and country can afford.
The term appo appropriate signifies that besides being scientifically
sound, the technology is also
- effective
- acceptable to those who apply it
- acceptable to those who use it
- in keeping with the Local culture
- simple to design and use
- capable of further development

5>
FOCUS ON PREVENTION
He
Health services should not only be curative but also should promote
health and healthy lifestyle with emphasis on prevention. Loop
@THE BEARDEDBONG

CONCEPTS OF PREVENTION
The goal of prevention is to promote health to preserve health, to
restare health when it is impaired and to minimise suffering and
distress.
Prevention can be studied under 3 Levels -
a> Primary prevention
b> Secondary prevention
c> Tertiary prevention
1> PRIMARY PREVENTION
It is defined as action taken prior to the onset of disease, which remove
the possibility that a disease will ever occur.
APPROACHES FOR PRIMARY PREVENTION
a> Primordial Prevention - It is the prevention of the emergence or
development of risk factors in countries or population groups in
which they have not yet appeared.
b> Population Strategy - It is directed at the whole population irrespec-
tive of individual risk levels so as to bring about behavioral and
lifestyle changes.
c> High risk strategy - It aims to bring preventive care to indivi-
- -duals at special risk.
These three approaches are complimentary and
should be implemented together to have an impact
on the population.

MODES OF INTERVENTION
Intervention is any attempt to intervene or interrupt the usual sequence
in the development of disease in man.
The modes of intervention in primary prevention are -
a> Health Promotion - It includes -
- Health education
- Environmental modifications
- Nutritional interventions
- Lifestyle and behavioral changes
b> Specific protection - It For eg -
- Fluorides and pit and fissure sealants far caries
prevention.
03
Advantages of primary prevention
- Low cast
II
Safe
-
The individual is notexpased
DBONG
to pain and suffering
2> SECONDARY PREVENTION
It is defined as action which halts the progress of a disease at its
incipient stage and prevents complications.
The modes of intervention are -
a> Early Diagnosis
b> Adequate treatment
Advantages
- important in reducing high martality and marbidity
of certain diseases Like hypertension
Disadvantages
- expensive

3> TERTIARY PREVENTION
It is defined as all measures available to reduce impairments
and disabilities, minimise suffering caused by existing dep-
- artures from good health and to promote the patients adjust
ments to irremediable conditions.
Disease
Impairment
Handicap
Disability
The modes of intervention are -
a> Disability Limitation
- Its objective is to prevent or halt the transition
of the disease process from impairment to
handicap.
b> Rehabilitation ARDEDBONG
- It is the combined and coardinated of medical,
vocational, social and educational measures for
training & retraining the individual to the highest
passible level of functional ability.

EPIDEMIOLOGY
DEFINITION
Epidemiology is defined as the study of the distribution and determinants
of health related states or events in specialised fied populations, and
the it application of this study to the control of health problems. (Last)
The word epidemiology is derived from the Greek word, Epidemic.
Epi = upon Demos availation!! = people, Logas = study or science
TraN&
PRINCIPLES OF EPIDEMIOLOGY
1. Exact Observation
( Strict, Vigarous, Accurate, Precise)
@T
2 Correct Interpretation
(Free from Error) DEDBONG
3. Rational Explanation
( Intelligent Sensible, Reasonable)
4. Scientific Construction
(By expert knowledge and technical skill)

SCOPES OF EPIDEMIOLOGY
According to Marris there are 7 scopes/uses of epidemiology
A>, To study historically the rise and fall of disease in the population
- since the health and disease pattern in a community is never
constant and there are fluctuations both over short and long
periods of time.
- As old diseases are conquered, and new ones have been identi-
- fied.
- Epidemiology provides a means to study disease profiles &
time trends in human poputation, thus making useful
projections into the future and identifying emerging health
problems.
B> Community Diagnasis
a Community diagnosis refers to the identification and
quantification of health problems in a community so as
to define those individuals or groups in need of health care.
- Quantification of health problems
trop
i> helps in laying down priorities in disease control
and prevention
ii> helps in evaluation of health services at a later date
iii> acts as a source of new knowledge about disease
distribution, causation and prevention
c> Planning and Evaluation
- Planning is essential for a rational allocation of the limited
resources.
- Epidemiologic information about the distribution of health
problems over time and place provides the basis for planning

health services.
- Evaluation is done to find out whether the measures undertaken
to control or prevent a disease are effective in reducing the frequency
of disease.
D> Evaluation of Individual's risk and chances
- Modern analytical epidemiology enables us to predict an individuals
chances far developing a disease or health related event.
- Besides the incidence rates and specific rates which are measures of
absolute risk, epidemiologists also calculate relative risk and attri-
butable risk for factors related to disease etiology.
E> Syndrome Identification
- Syndromes are identified by observing frequently associated findings
in individual patients.
- The classification of clinical syndromes, which include all signs and
symptoms that occur together and characterise a disease, carbe
accomplished by studies of all the aspects of disease and its atten-
dant circumstances to distinguish similar disorders and aid in
differential diagnosis.
F> Completing the natural history of disease
- Epidemiology is concerned with the entire spectrum of disease in
a population.
- The epidemiologist by studying disease patterns in the community
in relation to agent, hast and environmental factors is in a
better position to fill up the gaps in the natural history of disease
than the clinician.
G> Searching far causes/risk factors
- Epidemiology by relating disease to various attributes of the

population tries to identify the cause of disease and in the case
of chronic disease, epidemiology tries to identify the risk factor, N
paving the way for prevention of the cause or risk factor in
the future.
@THE THE BEARDEDBONG

TOOLS OF MEASUREMENT IN EPIDEMIOLOGY
The most commonly used tools of measurement in epidemiology are -
1> Rate
2> Ratio
3> Proportion
A> RATE
- A rate is the frequency of a disease or characteristic expressed per
unit size of the population or group in which it is observed.
- The time at or during which the cases are observed is a further speci-
fication needed for epidemiologic purposes.
- It is obtained by collecting the number of cases of a particular condition
(numerator) and dividing this figure by the total population at risk
00001
(denominator).
Role
No of events (deaths or
disease) in a specified period
n
a
Rate =
X
10
G
Population at risk of experiencing
the event or disease
- The denominator is commonly called the related or reference
population.
- In rate, numerator also forms a part of the denominator.
TYPES OF RATES
i> CRUDE RATES - These rates are the actual observed rates
such as the birth and death rates.
ii> SPECIFIC RATES - These rates are the actual observed rates
of diseases due to specific causes or diseases
occuring in specific groups or diseases during
specific time periods.

iii> Standardized Rates - These rates are obtained by direct or
indirect methods of standardisation or
adjustment like the age and sex stan-
dardised rates.
B> RATIO
- Ratio denotes the relation in size between two random quantities.
- In a ratio the numerator is not a part of the denominator.
- It is expressed as the result of one quantity divided by the other
and is represented as A:B or A
B
Eg - Dentist- Population ratio in a state in India is 1: 10000,
which means far every 10000 denti people there is
one dentist
OPTHE BEARDEDBONG
- A proportion is a ratio which expresses the relation in magnitude
of a part of the whole.
- In a proportion the numerator is always a part of the denominator
- It is usually expressed in percentage
Eg
The no. of school children with
dental caries at a certain time
X 100
The total no. of children in the
school at the same time

METHODS OF EPIDEMIOLOGY
Methods of Epidemiology
Descriptive
Analytical
Experimental
Epidemiology
Epidemiology
Epidemiology

Cohart
Study
Study
Randomised
Non-
Controlled
Randomised

A> DESCRIPTIVE EPIDEMIOLOGY
First phase of any epidemiological investigation
These studies are concerned with the observation of the distribution
of the disease or any health related events in human populations &
the identification of the characteristics with which the disease or condi-
tion under study seems to be associated.
USES
Provides data with regard to the types of disease problems and their
magnitude in the community
Provides information on the etiology of disease and helps in the
formulation of an etiological hypothesis
Provides data required for the planning and evaluating preventive
and curative services
Leads the path for further research with regard to a particular
disease problem

STEPS INVOLVED
1. Defining the population to be studied
2. Defining the disease under study
3. Describing the disease in terms of
a> time
b> place
c> person
4. Measurement of the disease
5. Comparing with known indices
6. Formulating an etiological hypothesis
B/ ANALYTICAL EPIDEMIOLOGY
It is designed primarily to establish the causes of disease by investigating
association between exposure to a risk factor and the occurance of
disease.
a
It is the second major RF type
of epidemiological studies and it focuses
on the individual within the population unlike descriptive epidemio-
logy which focuses on the entire population.
It is of 2 types - a> Case-control study
b> Conl Cohort study
FEATURES OF CASE CONTROL STUDY
a> both exposure and outcome (disease) have occured before
the start of the study
b> the study proceeds backwards from effect to cause
c> it uses a control or comparison group to support or refute
an inference
FEATURES OF COHORT STUDY
a> the cohorts are identified prior to appearance of the disease
under investigation

b> the study groups are observed over-o period of time to determine
the frequency of disease among them
c> the study proceeds forward from cause to effect
STEPS - CASE CONTROL STUDY
1. Selection of cases and controls
2. Matching
3. Measurement of exposure
4. Analysis and Interpretation
STEPS - COHORT STUDY
1. Selection of study subjects
2. Obtaining data on exposure
3. Selection of comparison groups.
4. Follow up
5. Analysis
c> EXPERIMENTAL REARDEDBONG
These studies involve some intervention or manipulation in the
experimental group while making no change in the control group
and observing and comparing the outcome of experiment in both
the groups.
It is of 2 types - a> Randomised Controlled Trials
b> Non randomised /non-experimental trials
Experimental or Intervention studies are carried out under the
direct control of the investigator.
USES
To provide scientific proof of etiological factors which may permit
the modification or control of those diseases
To provide a method of measuring the effectiveness and efficiency
of health services for the prevention, control and treatment of
disease and improve the health of community.

DENTAL PUBLIC HEALTH
DEFINITION
The science and art of preventing and controlling dental diseases.
and promoting dental health through organised community efforts.
It is that form of dental practice which serves the community
as a patient rather than an individual.
It is concerned with the dental health education of the public,
with applied dental research and with the administration of
group dental care programs as well as the prevention and control
of dental diseases on a community basis.
TOOLS OF DENTAL PUBLIC HEALTH
1. EPIDEMIOLOGY
a
It is defined as the study of the distribution and determinants
of health related states or events in specified populations and
the application of this study to the control of health problems.
- The aims of epidemiology are to minimise or eradicate the disease
or health problem and its consequences and to promote the well
being of society as a whole.
2. BIOSTATISTICS
- Statistics is the science of compiling classifying and tabulating
numerical data and expressing the results in a mathematical
or graphical form.
- Biostatistics is that branch of statistics concerned with mathe.
matical facts and data relating to biological events
- The uses of biostatistics are
a> to define normalcy

b> to test whether the difference between 2 populations' regarding
a particular attribute is real or a chance occurance such
c> to study the carrelation or association between two or more
attributes in the same population
d> to evaluate the efficacy of vaccines, sera etc. by case control
studies
e> to Locate, define and measure the extent of marbidity and
martality in the community
f> to evaluate the achievements of public health program
3. SOCIAL SCIENCES
- Social sciences usually include sociology cultural anthropology and
psychology.
- The public health worker, in a community health program, is very
dependent upon the group behaviour of the individuals, determined
by their culture.
Thus in case of a mismatch between the effort & the outcome of the
program, social scientists are called in to access the process and
find & help rectify the drawbacks.
- They aid in adapting new health programs to existing cultural
patterns.
4. PRINCIPLES OF ADMINISTRATION
- There are two main areas into which administrative work may be
divided - Organisation and Management
- Organisation deals with the structure of an agency and the way
people are arranged into working groups within it.
- Management is concerned with the handling of personnel and
operations in such a way that the work of the agency gets done.

5. PREVENTIVE DENTISTRY
- There are 3 levels of prevention - primary, secondary & tertiary
seaw - Primary prevention includes - Health Promotion and Specific
protection
- Secondary prevention ats involves - Early diagnosis stould and prompt
treatment
- Tertiary prevention involves - Disability Limitation and
Rehabilitation
@THE BEARDEDBONG

STEPS IN DENTAL PUBLIC HEALTH
1. SURVEY
- Surveys are methods for collection of data, analysing and evaluating
them in order to determine the amount of disease problems. in a comm-
unity and also to identify cases that have not been identified.
- Various parameters assessed in a survey include -
a> socio economic status of the community
b> nature of distribution of the community
c> health problems of the community
d> resources available for eliminating the problem
e> attitudes of the community towards oral health
- The focus of attention in a survey is the population rather than an
individual
2. ANALYSIS
Information collected through a survey is subjected: to an analysis
in order to define the characteristics of specific health problems in
the community.
- Since in dental public health, the dentist is interacting with groups
of people, it is necessary to organise the data to make the analysis
easier
- The science of statistics is applied to arrive at the common descriptive
constants Like the mean, median mode and standard deviation in
order to achieve a correct diagnasis or to arrive at an accurate
analysis.
PROGRAM PLANNING
- After the problem and its characteristics are analysed the next
step is program planning
- The main objective of the public health professional is to have the
designed program accepted by the community and the Patter

should participate in all the steps involved.
- Here, it is the community that makes the decision of accepting or
rejecting the program
4. PROGRAM OPERATION
- When a specific public health program has to be adopted for a
community a public health team, which constitutes of profe-
ssionals in various disciplines, has to be employed for executing
the program.
- Far example a community water fluaridation program will
require dentists to prepare baseline data regarding the relation
between NONT fluoride content and prevalance of caries along with
engineers and public works department who will be responsible
for addition of fluorides to water supply and periodical main-
tenance of equipments.
5. FINANCING BEARDEDBONG
- Before starting the public health program the health professional
have to identify the source for securing the funds and also
should plan for management of the same.
- Financing for public health programs are usually provided
by the governments or Local authorities
6 PROGRAM APPRAISAL
- This is the final step in any public health program where the
effectiveness of the program is assessed.
- The dimensions used for program appraisal are efficiency
appropriateness adequacy passible side effects etc
- The public health dentist is accountable to the community
for the periodic appraisal of the program.

POTABLE WATER
Potable water has been defined as water that is
a> free from pathogenic agents
b> free from harmful chemical substances
c> pleasant to taste i.e free from color and odor
d> useful for all domestic needs
WATER PURIFICATION ON SMALL SCALE
A> BOILING
The water must be brought to a rolling boil for 5-10 minutes
It kills all bacteria, spares, cysts and ova
It removes temparary hardness
It offers no residual protection DEDBONG
B> CHEMICAL DISINFECTION
Bleaching powder or chlorinated lime is a white amarphous
powder which contains about 33% available chlorine.
Chlorine solution is prepared by mixing 4 kg of bleaching powder
with 20 Litres of water to give a 5% solution of chlorine
High test hypochlorite or perchlaron is a calcium compound
which contains 60-70% available chlorine.
Chlorine tablets are good for disinfecting small quantities of
water but they are expensive.
Iodine may be used for emergency disinfection of water. A
contact time of 20-30 mins is needed far effective disinfection.
High cost is a major disadvantage.

Potassium permanganate may kill cholera vibrios. but is of
Little use against other disease arganisms.
It also alters the color, smell and taste of water and therefore
not used.
3> FILTRATION
Water can be purified on a small scale by filtering through
ceramic filters like Pasteur Chamberland filter, Berkefeld
filter.
@THE THE BEARDEDBONG

DOUBLE POT METHOD
Wells are the main source of water supply in the rural areas.
The most effective and economical method of disinfecting well is by
bleaching powder.
The double pot method is one such method of disinfection of wells.
Devised by National Environmental Engineering Research Institute,
Nagpur, India.
STRUCTURE
This methods uses 2 cylindrical pots, one placed inside the other.
The inside height and diameter of the outer pot are 30 cm and 25 cm
respectively.
A hole 1 cm in diameter is made in each pot.
In the inner pot the hole is in the upper portion, near the rim and in
the outer pot it is 4 cm above the bottom
@THE BEARDEDBONG
FUNCTIONING
Mixture of 1 kg bleaching powder and 2 kg coarse sand is prepared
and slightly moistened with water.
The inner pot is filled with with this mixture upto 3 cm below the
Level of the hole.
The inner pot is introduced into the outer one, & the mouth of the
latter is closed with polyethylene foil.
The double pot is Lowered into the well by means of a rope.
The pot should be immersed at least 1 m below the water Level to
prevent damage by the buckets used for drawing water.
This method can be used satisfactorily for 2-3 weeks in a well
containing about 4500 Litres of water.

WATER PURIFICATION ON LARGE SCALE
The components of a water purification system comprise of
A> Starage
B> Filtration
c> Chlorination
A> STORAGE
During starage, considerable amount of purification takes place by
1> Physical action - About 90% of the suspended impurities settle
down in 24 hours by gravity. The water becomes clearer allowing
penetration of Light.
2> Chemical action - The aerobic bacteria oxidise the organic
matter present in the water with the help of dissolved oxygen
reducing the content of free ammonia and increasing the conc-
of nitrates.
3> Biological action - During starage the pathogenic organisms
gradually die out. However, if the water is stared for Long periods,
there is Likelihood of development of vegetable growths such as
algae, which imparts bad smell and color to the water.
B> FILTRATION
Filtration is the second stage in the purification of water
The two types of filters used are -
1> Biological or Slow sand filter
2> Mechanical or rapid sand filter
SLOW SAND FILTERS
Elements of Slow sand Filter
1> Supernatant water - The depth of the supernatant water above
the sand bed varies from 1 to 1.5.m.
It provides a constant head of water so as to overcome the resistan
-ce of the filter bed.

It provides waiting period of some hours for the raw water to
undergo purification by sedimentation, oxidation and particle
agglomeration.
2> A bed of Graded Sand - The thickness of the sand bed is about
1 m. The sand grains have a diameter between 0.2-0.3. mm.
The sand bed is supported by a layer of graded gravel 30-40 cm
deep which also prevents the fine grains being carried into drainage
pipes.
Water percolates through the sand bed very slowly and it is subjec-
ted to mechanical straining, sedimentation, , absorption, oxidation
and bacterial action.
The rate of filtration lies between 0.1-0.4m³/hour/m² of sand
bed surface.
a
Vital Payer - slimy growth covering the surface of sand
bed is called as Schmutzdecke or vital Layer.
This Layer consists of threadlike algae, plankton, diatoms
and bacteria.
It takes several days for the vital layer to form fully &
when fully formed it extends for 2-3 cm into the top
partion of sand bed.
The formation of vital layer is called ripening of the
filter.
The vital layer is the heart of the slow sand filter. It
removes organic matter, holds back bacteria & oxidises
ammoniacal nitrogen into nitrates
3> An under drainage system 1 1 At the bottom of the filter bed is
the under drainage system. It consists of perforated pipes which
provides an outlet for filtered water 1 and also supports the filter
medium above.

4> A system of Filter control valves - They maintain a constant rate
of filtration. The venturimeter is used to measure the bed resistance
or lass of head.
When the resistance builds up the operator opens the regulating valve
so as to maintain a steady rate of filtration.
RAPID SAND FILTERS
The steps involved are
1> Coagulation - the raw water is first treated with a chemical coagulant
such as alum.
2> Rapid Mixing — the treated water is then subjected to violent agitation
in mixing chamber for few minutes.
This allows a quick and thorough dissemination of
alum throughout the bulk of water
3> Flocculation - this phase involves a slow and gentle stirring of the
THE
treated water in a flocculation chamber for 30 mins.
This results in formation of thick, white, flocculent
precipitate of aluminium hydroxide.
4> Sedimentation - The coagulated water is then into sedimentation
tanks where it is kept for 2-6 hours, when the floccu-
lent precipitate together with impurities and bacteria
settle down in the tank
5> Filtration The partly purified water is then subjected to rapid
sand filtration.
Filtration removes the remaining alum floc not
removed by sedimentation.
Back Washing - As filtration proceeds, the suspended
impurities and bacteria clog the filters resulting in their
reduced efficiency called lass of head.
Due to Loss of head, rapid sand filters need frequent
washing. Washing is done by reversing flow of water
through the sand bed c/a back washing.

c> CHLORINATION
Effects of Chlorination
chlorine kills pathogenic bacteria, but it has no effect on spares
and certain viruses except in high doses
It oxidises iron, manganese and hydrogen sulphide
It destroys taste and odour producing constituents
It controls algae & slime organisms
It aids coagulation
Mechanism of Action
When chlorine is added to water there is formation of hydro-
chloric acid and hypochlarous acid. The hydrochlaric acid is
neutralised by the alkalinity of water.
The hypochlarous acid ionises to form H ions and hypochlorite
ions. The disinfecting action of chlorine is due to hypochlarous
@THE HE BEARDEDBONG
Method Used
Chlorine is applied either as -
Chlorine gas 1 first choice because its economical efficient &
easy to apply. However, it is an irritant to the
eyes and poisonous
Chloramine - loase compounds of chlorine and ammonia.
They have less tendency to produce chlorinous
taste,
Perchlaron - It is also called high Test hypochlarite.

DISPOSAL OF SOLID WASTE
There is no single method of refuse disposal, which is usuall equally
suitable in all circumstances. The principal methods are-
A> BUMPING
In this method refuse is dumped in low lying areas.
This is an easy method of disposal of dry refuse and is also suitable
for reclamation of Land.
As a result of bacterial action, refuse decreases considerably in
volume and is converted gradually into humus.
Drawbacks of Open dumping
i> the refuse is exposed to flies and rodents
ii> it is a source of nuisance because of smell and
unsightly appearance
iii> the Loase refuse is dispersed by the action of wind
iv> drainage from dumps contribute to the pollution
ONG
of surface and ground water
It is considered as the most unsanitary method of waste disposal
B> CONTROLLED TIPPING
It is the most satisfactory method of refuse disposal where suitable land
is available.
In this method, the material is placed in a trench or other prepared
area, adequately compacted and covered with earth at the end of
the working day
Three methods are used in this operation
i> trench method - its used where Level ground is available
ii> ramp method - its used where Terrain is moderately sloping
iii> area method - this method is used for filling Land
depressions.

chemical, bacteriological and physical changes occur in the buried
refuse.
The temp. rises to 60°C within 7 days and kills all the pathogens
and hasten the decompasition process.
It takes 4-6 months for complete decomposition of innocuous
mass
c> INCINERATION
Refuse can be disposed off hygienically by burning or incineratio.
Incineration requires a preliminary separation of dust or ash
from the refuse.
It involves heavy outlay and expenditure, besides manipulative
difficulties.
Therefore, it has limited application in refuse disposal and is
D> COMPOSTING
@THE BEARDEDBONG
mainly used for healthcare waste disposal.
It is a process of nature where matter breaks down under
bacterial action resulting in the formation of a relatively stable
humus- Like material called the compast.
The principal by -products are carbon dioxide, water and heat.
The heat produced during compasting destroys all pathogenic
agents.
(>60°C) (>
The compost formed contains few or no disease producing organisms
and is a good soil - builder containing small amounts of major
plant nutrients such as nitrates & phasphates
The methods of composting are
i> Bangalare Method - Anaerobic method
- developed by ICAR at IIS, Bangalore
ii> Mechanical composting - aerobic method
- compast produced on Large scale

E> MANURE PITS.
They are dug by individual householders to dump the garbage cattle
dung straw & leaves
They are covered with earth after each day's dumping
In 5-6 months time, the refuse is converted into manure which can
be returned to the field.
This method of refuse disposal is effective and relatively simple in
rural communities.
F> BURIAL
A Trench is excavated and at the end of each day the trench is
filled with earth and compacted.
@THE BEARDEDBONG

HEALTHCARE WASTE MANAGEMENT
Health care waste is defined as all the waste generated by healthcare
establishments, research facilities and Laboratories.
A> INCINERATION
Incineration is a high temperature dry oxidation process that reduces
organic and combustible waste to inarganic, incombustible matter
and results in a very significant reduction of waste volume and
weight.
This process is usually used to treat wastes that can't be recycled
reused or dispased off in a Landfill site.
Types of Incinerators
i> Double chamber pyrolytic incinerators
ii> Single-chamber furnaces with static grate
iii> Rotary kilns BEARDEDBONG
Pyrolytic incinerators are the most reliable and commonly used for
health care waste, but they are expensive.
Single chamber incinerators are used when pyrolytic incinerators
can't be afforded.
B> CHEMICAL AISINFECTION
In this method, chemicals are added to the waste to kill or inactivate
the pathogens.
This method is most suitable for treating liquid waste such as
blood, urine, stools or hospital sewage.
Solid wastes and highly hazardous health care wastes may also
be disinfected chemically, with the following limitations -
i> shredding of waste is usually necessary before disinfection
ii> powerful disinfectants are required which are themselves
hazardous

iii> only the surface of intact solid waste will be disinfected
Types of Disinfectants
i> Farmaldehyde
ii> Ethylene oxide
iii> Glutaraldehyde
iv> Sodium hypochlarite
v> Chlorine Dioxide
c> WET THERMAL TREATMENT
Wet thermal treatment or steam disinfection is based on the exposure of
shredded infections waste to high temp, high pressure steam
The process requires that waste be shredded before treatment to
increase the disinfection efficiency.
Advantages
i> relatively Low investment and operating costs
ii> Low environmental BEARDEDBONG impact
Disadvantages
i> the shredder is liable to mechanical failure and breakdown
ii> the efficiency of disinfection is very sensitive to operational
conditions
iii> not applicable for treatment of anatomical wastes & animal cara
carcasses.
autoclaving is an effective wet thermal disinfection process.
D> MICROWAVE IRRADIATION
Most microorganisms are destroyed by the action of microwaves of a
frequency of 2450 MHz and a wavelength of 12.24 cm.
The water contained within the wastes is rapidly heated by the
microwaves and the infections components are destroyed by heat
conduction.
Disadvantages are high cast and operational problems.

E> ENCAPSULATION
This procedure involves filling containers made of high density
polyethylene or metal drums with waste.
These containers are then filled up with a medium of immobilising
material such as plastic foam cement mortar or clay.
After the medium has dried, the containers are sealed and
dispased off in Landfill sites.
F> SAFE BURYING
Safe burial of waste may be used when it is the only viable option
available
Access to the dispasal site should be restricted to authorised
personnel only
Large quantities of chemical waste should not be burried at one time
to avoid environmental pollution.
G> INERTIZATION BEARDEDBONG
The process involves mixing waste with cement and other substan-
ces before disposal in order to minimise the risk of toxic substan-
ces contained in the waste migrating into surface water or
ground water.
Suitable for pharmaceuticals and for incineration ashes with
high metal content.

HEALTH EDUCATION
Health education is a process that informs, motivates and help people
to adopt and maintain healthy practices and lifestyles advocates
environmental changes as needed to facilitate this goal and conducts
professional training and research to the same end.
PRINCIPLE S OF HEALTH EDUCATION
A> CREDIBILITY
It is the degree to which the message is perceived as trustworthy by the
receiver.
It should be scientifically proven, based on facts and should be
compatible with local culture and social goals:
B> INTEREST
Wisten to it
If the topic of health education is of interest to the people, they will
The health educator should identify the felt needs of the people &
then prepare a program that they can actively participate in to
make it successful.
DUICK Y8
c> PARTICIPATION
bigine
The health educator should encourage people to participate in the
program.
Methods Like group discussion, panel discussion etc. provide
opportunities far people's participation.
ANT
D> MOTIVATION
Motivation can be defined as fundamental desire for learning
in an individual.
will
Motives are of 2 types - primary motives (inborn desires)
- secondary motives (desires from
outside sources)

Health education can be facilitated by the motivation provided by
the desire to achieve individual goals. For eg in a teenager,
esthetics might be a motive to take care of his/her own teeth.
E> COMPREHENSION
It refers to the Level of understanding of the people who receive
health education
The health educator should first determine the level of Literacy
and understanding of the audience & act accordingly
Usage of Technical or medical terms, which are not familiar with
the common people should be avoided.
F> REINFORCEMENT
This is the principle that refers to the repetition needed in health
education.
It is not passible for people to learn new things in a short period of
time, so repetition at regular intervals help people to understand
better.
It is also called as booster dase' in health education.
G> LEARNING BY DOING
Just by listening to new ideas or seeing new things, it might be
difficult to implement them.
If the learning process is accompanied by doing the new things,
it is better instilled in the minds of the people
This principle is based on the proverb
if I hear, I forget; if I see, I remember; If do I know"
H> KNOWN TO UNKNOWN
Before the start of any health education program, the health
educator should find out how much the people already know
and then give them the new knowledge.

The existing knowledge of the people can be used as the basic step upon
which new knowledge can be placed.
I> SETTING AN EXAMPLE
The health educator should follow what he preaches
Eg - an educator highlighting the ill effects of tobacco should not be
seen smoking as it sends the wrong message to public.
J> GOOD HUMAN RELATIONS,
This principle states that the health educator should have good/friendly
relations with the people
He/she should have a kind and sympathetic attitude towards people
and should always be helpful to them in clarifying their doubts.
People
K> FEEDBACK
For any program to be successful it is necessary to collect a feedback
to find out if any modifications are needed to make the program
more effective.
L> COMMUNITY LEADERS
Community Leaders can be used to reach the people of the community
and to convince them about the need for health education.
They act as a medium of communication between the health
educator and local Deople.
